summary: Joel Spolsky shares a brief, informal post linking to a reader's question about e-commerce interface design and then takes a nostalgic dive through his own early internet history. With Google having made 20 years of Usenet archives searchable, Joel digs up his earliest posts dating back to 1988. He traces his online footprint from his first Usenet post as a Penn undergrad, through early open source contributions and GPL software releases, to his time at Microsoft and eventual move to New York.
key_points: Joel highlights a reader's question about e-commerce UI design, praising the quality of his readership's responses
Google's Usenet archive enables a personal archaeology of one's earliest internet presence dating back decades
Joel establishes his open source credentials by showing GPL contributions from 1988, predating many of his critics
The post traces Joel's digital journey across institutions: Penn, Yale, Microsoft, and New York
money_quotes: Google now has 20 years of Usenet online. You can entertain yourself with some scary stuff from my past.
More than a decade before Joel on Software I had things to say. Not very interesting things though.
Now the Slashdot kiddies have to shut up; I was writing GPLed software before they were born, bwa ha ha!
This was rather a difficult trick because Penn didn't officially allow undergrads to post to Usenet in those days (so I transferred out!)
key_insight: Joel uses newly searchable Usenet archives to playfully establish his long internet pedigree, showing that his instinct to share opinions and contribute to open source predated the modern blogging era by over a decade.
